text: So after undergoing a barium swallow, which was negative, we decided to take the patient to the OR to resect the cyst.
So the approach here is right side up, left lateral approach, and we're utilizing a three-port VATS technique.
The alexis port is placed posteriorly to give us a working port in the posterior aspect of the mediastinum, and it's at the fifth intercostal space.
We have a 10 millimeter port at the fifth intercostal space anteriorly, which we're using exclusively for lung retraction.
And I think you can see the gauze there anteriorly.
And finally, we have a 10 millimeter camera in the seventh intercostal space in the mid-axillary line.
As you can see, we're using a grasper to grab the cyst wall and a gentle peanut dissection throughout most of the procedure.
I'd just like to highlight there are edited

parts of this procedure that requires sharp dissection with the harmonic scalpel but most of those images had smoke interference so I'd edit them out but most of the dissection is blunt now if you look carefully we're at the inferior pulmonary vein now and we're just dissecting gently to get the inferior pulmonary vein free off the cyst wall you can see it's quite dense it's quite thick and the atria is just behind us

again we check posture you make sure that we're not poking hole into anything once we're able to safely connect both sides were able to lift the cyst upward and superiorly up to the airway we're just about to do that right now

you could tell from the amount of tension that we're applying that the adhesions are quite dense now I'd like to highlight that during the procedure we do have a flexible esophagus scope and flexible bronchoscope which is being utilized by a second surgeon during the OR and I'll show you that in just a moment now you'll see the bronchus on the right side here
And we're dissecting the sac off it.
You can see the dense adhesions onto the bronchus.
And because adhesions are so dense, we ended up fracturing some of the cartilages, gentle fractures there.
There isn't much injury internally because we're checking continuously.
So we know that we're good in terms of airway.

now when we continue the procedure I just want to highlight to you the use of the esophagus scope and how it's important to demarcate the posterior part of the wall from the esophagus which wasn't very easy and you have to make sure you don't injure the esophagus and we did the same thing with a bronchoscope at the same time at this point we continue the procedure upward
And now we're at the carina and you can see the left main bronchus through the right side.
As we retract the cyst, the carina is coming up with us with the subcarinal lymph nodes and the sac all in one piece.
Sometimes in these cysts they could be calcified or densely adherent to these structures.
And it might not be safe to take them out, so you might have to resect part of the cyst wall and leave some inside and cauterize them.
And there's nothing wrong with that.
But in this instance we were able to take out the whole cyst.
We perform an underwater test to make sure that there's no bronchial leak.
There's a little bit of air leak from the parenchyma from the posterior part of the dissection, but nothing from the bronchus.
We had the option of putting a mediastinal drain, but we opted to close the mediastinal space with two stitches and leave a pleural drain.
We felt that that would be better because of the deep mediastinal dissection.
Again, I'm going to just highlight to you some anatomy, and this is the carina.
You can see the right main and the left main from this side.
